rows.v: Pour-over or drip — methods that highlight clarity
Fruit pastries, yogurt, light breakfast fare
The "sour" taste some people associate with light roast is usually under-extraction, not the roast itself — a slightly longer brew time helps.
Drip coffee, French press — the most versatile roast
Buttered toast, banana bread, breakfast pastries
The "classic" coffee shop default — a safe order if you're unsure
French press, cold brew — stands up to ice and milk
Dark roasts actually have slightly less caffeine by volume than light roasts — the roasting process burns off some caffeine.
Brown sugar, baker's chocolate, charred wood
Espresso machines — high pressure extraction needs this roast's oils
"Espresso roast" describes the roast profile, not a bean type — any bean can be roasted for espresso.
The base for every drink below — a single shot is about 1oz (30ml). Order "a double" if you want more intensity without dilution.
Closest to drip coffee in strength, but made from espresso — a good order if you like black coffee and the café only has an espresso machine.
Espresso "stained" with a small dollop of foam
A true (Italian-style) macchiato is mostly espresso with a touch of foam — very different from the sweet, milky "caramel macchiato" at many chain cafés.
Equal parts espresso and steamed milk
A good middle ground if a cappuccino feels too foamy and a macchiato feels too strong.
Espresso, steamed milk, and a thick layer of foam — roughly equal thirds
The foam cap is the point. In Italy it's traditionally a morning drink, rarely ordered after a meal.
Espresso with steamed milk and a thin layer of microfoam
Stronger and less milky than a latte — usually made with a double shot in a smaller cup.
Espresso with a large amount of steamed milk and a thin layer of foam
The mildest and most customizable of the espresso drinks — the easiest to flavor with syrups if you want something sweet.
Espresso, steamed milk, and chocolate syrup or cocoa, topped with foam or whipped cream
Essentially a chocolate latte — a good gateway drink if you're not used to coffee's bitterness yet.
The foam on top comes from shaking hard with ice — a sign of a well-made one is a thick, persistent crema-like layer.
Hot coffee, Irish whiskey, sugar, cream float
The cream should be lightly whipped and floated, not stirred in — it should sit on top as you drink through it.
Cold brew, tonic water, ice, citrus garnish
A modern café favorite — the bitterness of tonic plays surprisingly well with the low-acid sweetness of cold brew.
Espresso shot poured over vanilla ice cream
Pour the espresso right at the table for the best contrast between hot and cold — don't let it sit and melt first.
Technically doesn't contain coffee itself — the coffee flavor comes entirely from the liqueur (e.g. Kahlúa).
